title: Memory leak in TaskFlow v2.3 causes progressive slowdown requiring daily restart
problem: After upgrading to TaskFlow v2.3, the server's memory usage climbs steadily from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ over the course of a day, causing page loads exceeding 10 seconds and API timeouts. The server requires a daily restart to recover. ~200 active users are affected.
root_cause_hypothesis: The 'improved real-time notifications' feature introduced in v2.3 is likely leaking WebSocket connections or accumulating notification state in memory. The high volume of WebSocket/push notification log entries toward end of day suggests connections are not being properly closed or cleaned up, leading to the observed memory leak.
reproduction_steps: Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a server with PostgreSQL backend
Allow normal usage by multiple users over the course of a full workday
Monitor memory usage via Grafana or similar — expect steady climb from ~500MB
Observe progressive slowdown in page loads and API response times as memory grows
environment: Ubuntu 22.04 VM, 8GB RAM, TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2), PostgreSQL, standard TaskFlow v2.3 runtime
impact: All ~200 active users experience degraded performance daily, with the application becoming effectively unusable by late afternoon. Requires manual daily restarts as a workaround.
recommended_fix: Investigate the v2.3 real-time notification system for WebSocket connection leaks. Check whether connections are properly closed on client disconnect, whether notification event listeners are being cleaned up, and whether any in-memory caches (e.g., per-connection notification queues) grow unbounded. Compare the notification subsystem code between v2.2 and v2.3 for changes that could cause accumulation. A heap dump comparison between startup and late-day would confirm the leaking objects.
proposed_test_case: Write a load test that simulates 200 users connecting and disconnecting WebSocket notification channels over several hours. Assert that memory usage remains within a bounded range (e.g., does not exceed startup + 500MB) and that closed connections are fully garbage collected.
information_gaps: Exact runtime version (Node.js/Python) — reporter unsure, but unlikely to change the investigation direction
Whether a v2.2 rollback confirms the issue is version-specific (reporter noted timing correlation but hasn't tested rollback)
Heap dump or memory profiler output identifying the specific leaking objects
